Commercial Apr 11, 2026

Separate Operations in Oman Air’s Acquisition of SalamAir

SalamAir has been acquired by Oman’s government as part of an integration process. According to the government, the acquisition aims to form a “sustainable national aviation sector,” though Oman Air and SalamAir will remain independent in both operations and branding for the time being. The origins...

Oman Air's acquisition of SalamAir creates a dual-brand strategy where separate operational independence may reduce synergies while consolidating government control of the national carrier ecosystem.

See Travel Radar →

Military Apr 11, 2026

Why The E-3 Sentry's Rotating Radar Dome Will Be Nearly Impossible To Replace

The eye in the sky the USAF is saying goodbye to.

The E-3 Sentry's phased-array radar technology integration into airframe structures makes modernization economically and technically prohibitive, forcing complete platform retirement rather than upgrades.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Apr 11, 2026

"It's Not A Game": FAA Targets Gamers As US Faces 3,500 Air Traffic Controller Shortfall

As the US faces a 3,500 air traffic controller shortfall, the FAA is making a bold move by targeting gamers for recruitment.

FAA's gaming-focused recruitment strategy recognizes that real-time decision-making skills from competitive gaming directly transfer to high-pressure air traffic control environments.

See Simple Flying →

General Aviation Apr 11, 2026

AOPA presents draft governance changes to members

AOPA presented a set of bylaw changes to members, seeking feedback on a set of significant potential revisions, including changes to the process for nominating members to the Board of Trustees, setting term limits, and establishing required trustee qualifications to serve on the Board.

AOPA's proposed term limits and qualification standards signal a shift toward professionalized governance that could reshape the organization's strategic direction and membership influence over aviation policy.

See AOPA News →

Aerospace Apr 11, 2026

NASA Welcomes Record-Setting Artemis II Moonfarers Back to Earth

The first astronauts to travel to the Moon in more than half a century are back on Earth after a record-setting mission aboard NASA’s Artemis II test flight. NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch, and CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen splashed down at...

Successful Artemis II return demonstrates NASA's readiness to execute lunar missions with international crew, establishing critical operational baseline for upcoming crewed Moon landings.

See NASA →

Safety Apr 11, 2026

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Organizations must document why residual risks remain acceptable after implementing controls, creating an auditable defense of safety decisions under regulatory scrutiny.

Military Apr 11, 2026

How The Eurofighter Typhoon Stacks Up Against The F-15, F-16, & F/A-18 In 2026

Fourth-generation fighters were supposed to disappear. Instead, they still define Western air power in 2026.

Fourth-generation fighter procurement remains strategically critical as fifth-generation production backlogs and costs force NATO allies to sustain and upgrade legacy platforms through the 2030s.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 11, 2026

10 Years From Now, This Is What Happens To Today’s Boeing 777 Fleet

Delays within the 777X program mean we could frequently be seeing older variants from the family for years yet.

Extended 777X delays are creating a longer operational window for aging 777 variants, requiring airlines to commit to costlier maintenance programs rather than accelerating fleet modernization timelines.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 11, 2026

Moog Technology Keeps Artemis II Astronauts Safe During Historic Lunar Mission

East Aurora, NY – Moog Inc. (NYSE: MOG.A and MOG.B), a worldwide designer, manufacturer, and systems integrator of high-performance precision motion and fluid controls and control systems, highlights the critical […]...

Moog's precision motion control systems represent the supply chain dependencies that make human spaceflight possible, with component reliability becoming increasingly central to mission-critical aerospace operations.

See SpaceNews →

Regulatory Apr 11, 2026

How to Assess Safety Risk

A practical walkthrough of safety risk assessment under 14 CFR Part 5.

Part 5's formal risk assessment framework enables operators to quantify and systematically mitigate hazards beyond minimum regulatory compliance.

Aerospace Apr 11, 2026

Orion splashes down to successfully end Artemis 2 mission

The first human mission beyond Earth orbit in more than 50 years successfully concluded with a splashdown in the Pacific Ocean April 10.

Artemis 2's successful return validates NASA's deep-space capsule design and recovery procedures essential for lunar missions, restoring confidence in human spaceflight beyond low Earth orbit.

See SpaceNews →

Commercial Apr 11, 2026

The World's Most Comfortable Premium Economy Doesn't Have The Most Legroom

Emirates won the premium economy seats category in the Skytrax awards, yet it has less legroom than that of Japan Airlines.

Comfort rankings now prioritize seat design and amenities over legroom alone, signaling airlines can differentiate premium economy through engineering rather than pitch expansion.

See Simple Flying →

International Apr 10, 2026

The Role of Aviation in Humanitarian Aid and Disaster Relief

The United Nations Humanitarian Air Service (UNHAS) managed by the World Food Programme (WFP) is expected to play a vital role in 2026, serving as a necessary link for aid workers and relief supplies in some of the world’s most remote and dangerous locations. What is Humanitarian Aviation?...

UNHAS operations in 2026 will expand critical access to isolated crisis zones where commercial infrastructure is absent, creating sustained demand for specialized cargo and personnel transport capabilities.

See Travel Radar →

General Aviation Apr 11, 2026

KSQL Noise Abatement Departure

The San Francisco Bay area is complicated airspace, and right in the middle of it is San Carlos Airport. San Carlos airport is surrounded by terrain, San Francisco International class bravo,...

San Carlos Airport's noise abatement procedures reflect how smaller regional airports must navigate complex airspace constraints while managing community relations in densely populated metropolitan areas.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Apr 10, 2026

Delta Air Lines Retires Its Oldest Widebody After Nearly 36 Years & 150,000 Flight Hours

The aircraft has operated continuously for the airline since the 1990's.

Delta's retirement of a 36-year-old widebody reflects accelerating fleet modernization cycles driven by fuel efficiency gains and maintenance costs of aging aircraft.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 10, 2026

Swedish Armed Forces Release Photo Showing JAS 39 Gripens Escorting Russian Kilo-Class Submarine

The Russian submarine shadowed by the Swedish Gripens was transiting the Kattegat, a strategic waterway linking the Baltic approaches with the North Sea system. Although the spotlight is currently on Iran, the Russian military activity in the Baltic region continues to draw scrutiny. The Swedish...

Sweden's demonstrated intercept capability signals heightened NATO-aligned surveillance operations in the strategically vital Baltic corridor, reflecting persistent Russian naval activities beyond headline-dominating conflicts.

See The Aviationist →

Air Cargo Apr 10, 2026

Cathay Cargo Launches New Manage Booking System

Cathay Cargo has upgraded its booking system, giving customers direct control over their bookings. This system, called Manage Booking, is available 24/7 on their online platform. The update has been made to allow for instant changes post-booking rather than needing to follow up with the company....

Self-service booking management reduces customer service overhead while improving operational flexibility for cargo operators managing high-volume shipments.

See Travel Radar →

▶
General Aviation Apr 11, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Eastern Iowa Airport, IA (CID)

Eastern Iowa Airport (CID) is a medium-sized multi-use airport located south of Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Traffic includes basic training aircraft up through air carrier aircraft. Flight schools often use...

Eastern Iowa Airport's mixed-use model serving flight training through commercial carriers demonstrates the economic viability of regional hubs balancing general and commercial aviation demand.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Air Cargo Apr 10, 2026

Why The Workhorse Of The World’s Cargo Airlines Is No Longer In Production

The final Boeing 747 was delivered in January 2023.

The 747's retirement marks a supply-chain vulnerability for cargo operators who relied on its 140-ton payload capacity, forcing airlines to retrofit older widebodies or adopt smaller aircraft combinations.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 10, 2026

Progress: Qantas’ 1st Airbus A350-1000ULR Rolls Out With New Rolls-Royce Engines

The first Qantas A350-1000ULR, a game-changer for ultra-long-haul flights, has been spotted with its new Rolls-Royce engines in Toulouse.

Qantas's A350-1000ULR deployment signals a shift toward ultra-long-haul profitability through advanced Rolls-Royce engines enabling non-stop transcontinental routes previously requiring fuel stops.

See Simple Flying →

General Aviation Apr 10, 2026

Virginia Airport Rebrand Draws Local Opposition

Manassas weighs adding “Washington” to airport name ahead of planned airline service.

Rebranding airports to attract commercial service requires navigating community identity concerns that can delay or derail route expansion strategies.

See AVweb →

Industry Apr 11, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Integrated operational optimization across airlines, airports, and air traffic systems offers greater environmental and capacity gains than single-sector improvements alone.

See MDPI →

Industry Apr 10, 2026

Social Security database added to Treasury’s fraud-checking system

Treasury’s Do Not Pay service, which helps agencies and states verify identities and eligibility before issuing payments, now includes the SSA’s massive Numident database.

Treasury's expanded fraud-detection system integrating Social Security data strengthens identity verification for aviation vendor payments and employee screening across the industry.

See FedScoop →

Military Apr 10, 2026

A Look At The Salaries Of US Air Force Pilots In 2026 & Why $50,000 Bonuses Still Can't Keep Them

The story behind the Air Force's depleted pilot ranks.

Despite $50,000 bonuses, Air Force pilot retention crisis reflects broader industry competition as commercial airlines and legacy carriers aggressively recruit experienced military aviators with superior compensation packages.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Apr 10, 2026

VIP Notice - Las Vegas, NV

Notice: Expect VIP movement April 16-17, 2026 in the vicinity of Las Vegas, NV . Pilots can expect temporary airspace restrictions in conjunction with this VIP movement. The FAA recommends that all aircraft operators check NOTAMs OFTEN for temporary airspace restrictions prior to operations within...

VIP movements trigger dynamic airspace restrictions requiring pilots to monitor NOTAMs continuously rather than relying on standard flight planning procedures.

See FAASafety.gov Notices →

Regulatory Apr 10, 2026

Transportation Department targets gamers in ATC hiring push

The agency is focusing on attracting early career professionals for the technical role as its annual hiring window for air traffic controllers draws near.

FAA's gaming community recruitment strategy signals recognition that controller candidates need advanced spatial reasoning and real-time decision-making skills comparable to complex video game mechanics.

See FedScoop →

Commercial Apr 10, 2026

Milestone 220: Airbus Celebrates 500 Deliveries Of Its “Game-Changing” A220

Airbus has reached 500 A220 deliveries, but challenges still loom.

Despite reaching 500 deliveries, the A220 still faces profitability challenges and competitive pressure from Boeing's 737 MAX in the narrow-body segment.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 10, 2026

Caribbean Airlines Posts $250M Loss Since Air Jamaica Takeover

Caribbean Airlines has incurred more than $250 million in losses since acquiring Air Jamaica in 2011. The debt is tied to long-standing financial challenges following the integration. Mounting Losses Continue to Add Up At the time, the acquisition was intended to solidify North American services by...

The Air Jamaica acquisition demonstrates how integration failures in airline M&A can destroy shareholder value, signaling that regional carriers must carefully evaluate synergy assumptions before major consolidations.

See Travel Radar →

Safety Apr 10, 2026

Circle-to-Land Approach Hazards

Circling approaches are a necessary part of general aviation operations due to certain airport environments or situations like unfavorable winds. However, circling approaches present an increased risk to general aviation operations because they require maneuvering at low altitude and low airspeed...

Circling approaches demand heightened pilot proficiency in low-altitude maneuvering where recovery margins from stalls or loss-of-control situations are critically compromised.

See FAASafety.gov Notices →

Picture of the Day: April 10, 2026

Heading up on a bright blue day.

Visual documentation of optimal flying conditions suggests increased operational efficiency and favorable weather patterns for aviation activities.

Regulatory Apr 10, 2026

EPA has major role to play in Trump’s ‘AI capital of the world’ plans

The White House wants to cut the EPA’s budget by more than half while giving the agency $200M+ for AI. Some wonder how that squares with its environmental mission.

Aviation operators should anticipate regulatory shifts as EPA's reduced environmental enforcement capacity may reshape emissions oversight and compliance timelines for fleet operations.

Aerospace Apr 10, 2026

HawkEye 360 files to go public

Based in Herndon, Virginia, the company specializes in space-based radio-frequency signals intelligence for government agencies...

RF geolocation satellite imagery could enable new aviation safety applications like real-time tracking of non-cooperative aircraft and emergency distress signal detection.

Commercial Apr 10, 2026

Best Airline Stopover Programmes for 2026

Long-haul travel is increasingly being repositioned by airlines as an opportunity for tourism rather than transit. Stopover programs allow passengers to extend layovers into short stays at little or no additional airfare, effectively combining two destinations in one journey. What Is a Stopover...

Airlines are monetizing extended layovers as value-added tourism experiences rather than viewing them as costly operational necessities, creating competitive differentiation in long-haul markets.

See Travel Radar →

Aerospace Apr 10, 2026

Spacety raises $190 million to scale satellite manufacturing, plans IPO

Chinese satellite maker Spacety has completed multiple rounds of equity financing worth $190 million to scale its vertically integrated satellite manufacturing and data services model.

Spacety's vertically integrated model demonstrates how satellite manufacturers are capturing value across production and data services, signaling a competitive shift in space-based infrastructure.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Apr 10, 2026

New Perspective of Home

Seen during Artemis II’s lunar flyby on April 6, 2026, the Moon and Earth align in the same frame, each partially illuminated by the Sun. The Moon’s surface appears in sharp detail in the foreground, while Earth sits much farther away, smaller and softly lit in the background. A faint reflection in...

Artemis II's lunar photography demonstrates advanced imaging capabilities that will document human spaceflight milestones and support future mission planning and public engagement.

See NASA →

Military Apr 10, 2026

Air Force Boosts FY26 Aviation Bonus Incentives

Program increases payouts for shorter commitments, with total contracts reaching up to $600,000.

Shorter service commitments with higher payouts signal Air Force urgency to address pilot retention amid competitive civil aviation recruitment.
